title_tesim: Cabaret group, The Revuers, featuring Judy Holliday, Adolph Green, Betty Comden and Alvin Hammer on the set of Greenwich Village
caption_tesim: A Scene from the 20th Century-Fox Production "Greenwich Village"
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Actress/singer and future Oscar-winner, Judy Holliday (birth name: Judith Tuvim), appears here in her first film, "Greenwich Village," credited only as a member of the cabaret group, The Revuers. Most of her scenes, including the scene depicted in this photograph, did not make the film's final cut.

The scene depicted in this photograph does not appear in the film, "Greenwich Village." The members of The Revuers, however, do appear briefly in a party scene in the apartment of actress Vivian Blaine's character, 'Bonnie Watson'. Two of The Revuers, Alvin Hammer and Adolph Green, are also seen singing the song,   "When You Wore a Tulip and I Wore a Big Red Rose," alongside actors, B. S. Pully, Don Ameche and William Bendix.

From the AFI listing: The picture marked the screen debut of The Revuers, a cabaret group featuring Judy Holliday (who is billed as Judith Tuvim on the CBCS), Betty Comden, Adolph Green and Alvin Hammer. Although a 16 Feb 1944 Hollywood Reporter news indicated that The Revuers' "satiric sketch of a Shubert operetta" had been purchased by the studio for their debut, their sequence was cut from the finished picture, and modern sources note that the group appears only in the party scene at "Bonnie Watson's" apartment. After the group broke up, Holliday became a well-known Broadway and motion picture comedienne and won an Academy Award for Best Actress for "Born Yesterday." Comden and Green became a popular songwriting team whose films included "On the Town."

Actress/singer/songwriter, Betty Comden, is credited as, 'Hat Check Girl,' and as a member of the cabaret group, The Revuers in the film, "Greenwich Village." Most of the Revuers' scenes, including the one depicted in this photograph, did not make the film's final cut.

title_tesim: Marta Toren, Lowell Gilmore, and Philip Friend in Sword In The Desert
uniform_title_tesim: Sword In The Desert, The  (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:From: Universal-InternationalUniversal City, Calif."SWORD IN THE DESERT" - A Pictorial SynopsisFirst movie laid against a background of modern Palistine, "Sword in the Desert" is one of the topical bannerline films of the year and will be subject to much discussion. Its rapid-fire story capsuled into a 48-hour period on Dec. 23-24, 1947, during the British mandate over Palestine, it is based on a series of factual incidents. Its conflict is not political argumentation between British and Jews, but a story of action - a chase involving British patrols, Jewish immigrants and underground fighters, an American freighter captain - and, of course, a girl. Starring Dana Andrews, Marta Toren and Stephen McNally, it was written and produced by Robert Buckner and directed by George Sherman. The story in pictures:7. The British reach the hideout only seconds after the fleeing trucks, all having escaped but Sabra, a highly-prized captive for Major Stephens (LOWELL GILMORE) and Lieut. Ellerton (PHILLIP FRIEND.) Miles out in the desert Vogel halts the caravan, finds Sabra is missing, blames Dillon and assaults him. Kurta separates them and calms Vogel.

title_tesim: Dana Andrews and Stephen McNally in Sword In The Desert
uniform_title_tesim: Sword In The Desert, The  (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:From: Universal-InternationalUniversal City, Calif.Sept 30 '49"SWORD IN THE DESERT" - A Pictorial SynopsisFirst movie laid against a background of modern Palistine, "Sword in the Desert" is one of the topical bannerline films of the year and will be subject to much discussion. Its rapid-fire story capsuled into a 48-hour period on Dec. 23-24, 1947, during the British mandate over Palestine, it is based on a series of factual incidents. Its conflict is not political argumentation between British and Jews, but a story of action - a chase involving British patrols, Jewish immigrants and underground fighters, an American freighter captain - and, of course, a girl. Starring Dana Andrews, Marta Toren and Stephen McNally, it was written and produced by Robert Buckner and directed by George Sherman. The story in pictures:1. Mike Dillon (DANA ANDREWS), American freighter captain, stands on the deck of his ship off the shore of Palestine in the pre-dawn darkness of Dec. 23, 1947. He has smuggled a cargo of refugees through the British blockade, but he feels no sympathy for the tattered people he has ordered into landing boats. He wants his price per head and to get his ship away, but is told by David Vogel (STEPHEN MCNALLY - hatless) that he will be paid only when the refugees are safe on shore.

It is the story of a young man unjustly condemned to die for the murder of his wife, of the despairing efforts of the girl who loves him to locate the "phantom" witness who can establish his innocence, and of the mad machinations of the real criminal in seeking to save himself from exposure.6. Convicted by a jury which refused to believe his unsupported alibi, Scott is sentenced to be executed. In prison he is visited by his former secretary, Carol Richman (ELLA RAINES), turns deaf ears on her urgings that he appeal.From: Universal Studios,Universal City, Calif."Phantom Lady," a tense mystery drama, is a Universal production starring Franchot Tone, Ella Raines and Alan Curtis, directed by Robert Siodmak with Joan Harrison as associate producer. title_tesim: Michel Thomas and Christine Schmidter at an industry screening of The Slender Thread
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: A special invitational screening of Paramount's The Slender Thread for opinion-making groups representing all communication media and motion picture and television arts and sciences, was held on the evening of December 8th, 1965, at the Screen Director's Guild Theatre in Hollywood, as a major phase of a campaign to qualify the film for "Oscar" nominations. Mr. Howard W. Koch, Vice President in Charge of Production and Studio Operations, hosted a gala supper party which followed immediately after the screening. Among those in attendance were Stephen Alexander, Producer of The Slender Thread and Telly Savalas and Steven Hill, co-stars of the film, which was directed by Sydney Pollack, from an original screenplay by Stirling Silliphant.In this photo: Michel Thomas and Christine Schmidtmer -- the latter was one of the feminine leads in Hal Wallis' Boeing Boeing.

title_tesim: Herbert Rudley, Thomas Gomez, Hugo Haas, Peter Lorre, and other unidentified actors in Casbah
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: "Casbah" - A Pictorial Synopsis"Casbah," a musical film based on the novel "Pepe LeMoko," is a Marston Production released by Universal-International. The picture co-stars Yvonne DeCarlo, Tony Martin and Peter Lorre and introduces Swedish newcomer Marta Toren. Tony Martin and Producer Nat Goldstone head the newly formed producing company. The songwriting team of Harold Arlen and Leo Robin composed five new songs which are introduced in "Casbah" by Tony Martin and Yvonne DeCarlo. The famed Katherin Dunham Dancers, Thomas Gomez, Hugo Haas and Douglas Dick are featured. John Berry directed. Nat Goldstone was the producer. The story in pictures:4. When Carlo's ruse, secretly organized by Inspector Slimane (Peter Lorre), (R), fails, Police Chief Louvain (Thomas Gomez) (C), organizes a search of the fortress casbah for Pepe. The gang leader is nabbed but freed by his loyal friends who ambush the posse.

title_tesim: Ted de Corsia, Edgar Hinton, Jerry Hartleben, and Charles Boyer in The Buccaneer
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: A Pirate's Trouble Homecoming -- A pirate's life is not always of the "yo, ho, ho and a bottle of rum" type, as one dramatic scene in Paramount's "The Buccaneer" plainly shows. In the story, based on the true life adventures of the fabulous pirate Jean Lafitte, the famous buccaneer has to put to sea in an attempt to prevent a renegade crew of his from attacking an American ship. He reaches the scene too late and is forced to hang the rebellious captain who has disobeyed his orders. Then comes the return to his headquarters at Barataria, one of the islands off the coast of New Orleans. Because the booty is the first ever taken from a United States vessel, he orders it stored away and not divided. Other pirate leaders of his are openly critical, especially when Lafitte brings back one survivor of the sunken ship, the cabin boy who could reveal the criminal story. And the daughter of the hanged pirate tries to kill Lafitte by hurling a knife at him. Thus is the homecoming of this pirate. Yul Brynner plays Lafitte and Claire Bloom the pirate girl. Other stars are Charlton Heston, Charles Boyer, Inger Stevens and Henry Hull. The picture, photographed in Technicolor and VistaVision, was produced by Henry Wilcoxon, directed by Anthony Quinn and supervised by Cecil B. DeMille.Dominique You (Charles Boyer) tells First Mate (Edward Hinton) and Rumbo (Ted de Corsia) that the pirate Brown left only Miggs (Jerry Hartleben), the cabin boy, as survivor from the sunken Corinthian.
"The Buccaneer"Produced by Henry Wilcoxon  Directed by Anthony Quinn  Supervised by Cecil B. DeMilleA Paramount Picture  In VistaVision  Color by Technicolor
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: (From left) Ted de Corsia, Edgar Hinton, Jerry Hartleben, and Charles Boyer in "The Buccaneer."

title_tesim: Robert F. Simon and cast members in a scene from The Buccaneer
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Pirates Scuttle a Ship -- Among the colorfully spectacular scenes in "The Buccaneer," Paramount's multi-million dollar production in Technicolor and VistaVision, depicts a pirate ship attacking an American ship, seizing all cargo of value and then sinking the vessl with no lives saved. Paramount built the American ship, the Corinthian, then burned much of it, as the pirate ship lies alongside. Hollywood was combed for physically powerful actors to portray the pirates since the entire scene employed violent action, with leaps between the two ships and climbing of the rigging. This scuttling of an American ship by a rebellious group of pirates under the fabulous Jean Lafitte was taken from the real-life of the pirate chief, played by Yul Brynner. It was this one act that proved disastrous to Lafitte after he'd become an American hero by aiding General Andrew Jackson, portrayed by Charlton Heston, at the Battle of New Orleans. Starring with these two in the production are Claire Bloom, Charles Boyer, Inger Stevens and Henry Hull. The film was produced by Henry Wilcoxon, directed by Anthony Quinn and supervised by Cecil B. DeMille.
"The Buccaneer"Produced by Henry Wilcoxon  Directed by Anthony Quinn  Supervised by Cecil B. DeMilleA Paramount Picture  In VistaVision  Color by Technicolor

title_tesim: Dick Powell tries to wriggle through the doggy door in a scene from You Never Can Tell
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: Universal-International, Universal City, Calif. "You Never Can Tell" --- A Pictorial Synopsis. "You Never Can Tell" is a movie for those who think they have seen everything. It is literally the doggondest comedy ever filmed, telling a story of the reincarnation of animals as humans. Starring Dick Powell and Peggy Dow, it is from a story by Lou Breslow, screenplay by Breslow and David Chandler, and was directed by Breslow. It was produced by Leonard Goldstein for Universal-International. The story in pictures: 4. Rex is in a dilemma. He can't go to Ellen and say, "I'm your dog, King, reincarnated. Your boy friend, Collins, killed me." But he wants to prove it. Denied entrance to Ellen's home by the butler, Rex knows where King's entrance in the kitchen door is, so he gets in that way.

title_tesim: Dorothy Howe in Cocoanut Grove
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Capes - are back in various versions for Hollywood's early summer fashion trend. Dorothy Howe in Paramount's "Cocoanut Grove" wears a daytime dress of brown jersey with an interesting belt of shiny gold kid. The neckline is collarless but cut in a shallow, scalloped "V" which is repeated at the hem of the dress. The sleeves are long and bloused below the elbow over a tight cuff. An unlined, circular cape of beige wool crepe fastens at one side of the neckline and is banded in mink. Brown accessories and a muff of mink complete the costume.

title_tesim: Robert E. Strickland, Mickey Rooney and Guy Kibbee in Girl Crazy
caption_tesim: Handwritten in black ink text from back of photograph: 172

Typed text from back of photograph: Mickey's In The Middle Again...This time it's in Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer's "Girl Crazy," that Mickey Rooney gets in and out of trouble faster than ever before. He's seen as the playboy son of a New York publisher who is sent to a small desert college for reformation purposes. There he meets his favorite co-star and screen sweetheart, Judy Garland. The film presents the songs by America's favorite composer, George Gershwin, played by Tommy Dorsey and his band. Mickey and Judy head a cast that includes Rags Ragland, Nancy Walker, Guy Kibbee, Gil Stratton, Robert Strickland and Frances Rafferty. The film was produced by Arthur Freed and directed by Norman Taurog.

title_tesim: Helen Traubel in Deep in My Heart
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Happy Times...Jose Ferrer as Sigmund Romberg in M-G-M's story of the composer and his music, "Deep In My Heart", has fun with his trio of lovely ladies, Merle Oberon, Helen Traubel and Doe Avedon. In between set-ups, Ferrer turns stillman to take their pictures, amuses them with color slides, explains the intricacies of miniature sets, and puts on a one-man show -- just exactly what he does on the screen -- singing, dancing, romanticizing in the all-star color musical which boasts  11 Romberg operettas, 21 Romberg songs, and 21 big-name stars. Stanley Donen directs, Roger Edens producers and Eugene Loring does the choreography.
